name: td life insurance
facts: Waivers or changes to the policy conditions must be clearly expressed in writing and signed by an officer of TD Life Insurance Company.
No legal action can be brought to recover on the policy before 120 days after proof of claim is furnished.
Actions or proceedings against an insurer for insurance money must be commenced within the time limits set out in the applicable legislation.
For actions governed by Alberta and British Columbia laws, the time limit is set out in the Insurance Act.
For actions governed by Manitoba laws, the time limit is set out in the Insurance Act.
For actions governed by Ontario laws, the time limit is set out in the Limitations Act, 2002.
For actions governed by Quebec laws, the prescriptive period is set out in the Civil Code of Quebec.
The insurance policy is governed by the laws of the province where the applicant was resident when initially applying for the policy.

You will receive the benefits described in the policy if you pay the premiums shown in the premium schedule.
You must pay all premiums by the premium due date.
If paying monthly, the first premium is due on the First Premium Due Date specified in the Policy Schedule.
Subsequent monthly premium due dates occur one calendar month after the previous due date.
If paying annually, the first premium is due on the First Premium Due Date specified in the Policy Schedule.
Subsequent annual premium due dates occur one year after the previous due date.
You may be eligible for a premium discount, which will be applied if applicable.
If you become ineligible for a premium discount, your premiums will be adjusted accordingly.
Any premium collected for insurance coverage after the date of death of the insured person will be refunded in the same manner as the Sum Insured.
You must notify the insurer if you wish to change the method or frequency of premium payments.
Your premium is set and guaranteed for each 20-year term of your insurance coverage.
Your premium will not change during each 20-year term, regardless of changes in your age or health.
Premium payments must be made when due to maintain coverage.
The policy will not lapse if premiums are paid on time.
Coverage automatically renews at the end of each 20-year term for another 20-year term.
No medical questions are required to renew coverage at the end of each 20-year term.
Coverage cannot be renewed beyond your 80th birthday.
The benefit amount from your insurance will be payable to the beneficiary(ies) you designated.
Beneficiaries can be your spouse, child, friend, or any other person you select.
If no beneficiary is designated, the benefit amount will be payable to the policy owner or the policy owner's estate as applicable.

You have a 30-day review period from your effective date of the original policy.
The review period allows you to review the benefits provided by the policy.
You can decide whether or not the coverage suits your needs during the 30-day review period.
If you decide to discontinue the policy during the review period, you must contact the provider.
No claim must have been made for a refund to be issued.
Any premium paid will be promptly refunded if the policy is cancelled during the review period.
The policy will be cancelled as of your effective date if cancelled during the review period.
All obligations and liabilities under the policy will end immediately when the cancellation request is received.
A spouse is a person legally married to another.
A child is a son or daughter of a parent.
A mother is a female parent.
A father is a male parent.
A brother is a male sibling.
A sister is a female sibling.
An aunt is a sister of one's father or mother.
An uncle is a brother of one's father or mother.
A cousin is a child of one's aunt or uncle.
A niece is a daughter of one's brother or sister.
A nephew is a son of one's brother or sister.
A grandmother is a mother of one's father or mother.
A grandfather is a father of one's father or mother.
A sister-in-law is the wife of one's brother or the sister of one's spouse.
A brother-in-law is the husband of one's sister or the brother of one's spouse.
A mother-in-law is the mother of one's spouse.
A father-in-law is the father of one's spouse.
A friend is a person with a close personal relationship.
An estate is a person's property and assets.
A revocable beneficiary designation allows the owner to change the beneficiary at any time.

A trustee holds legal title to property for the benefit of another person.
A trust is a legal relationship where a trustee manages property for the benefit of another.
The law does not allow insurers to pay benefits directly to minors.
Care should be taken when naming minor beneficiaries.

We will not waive premiums if total disability results from attempted suicide or self-inflicted injury.
Total disability resulting from war, whether declared or undeclared, will not result in premium waiver.
Total disability caused by the commission or attempted commission of a criminal act will not lead to premium waiver.
An accident occurring while operating a conveyance with a blood alcohol level exceeding the legal limit will not trigger premium waiver.
Taking a drug in a manner not prescribed by a licensed physician will not result in premium waiver.
Notice of total disability must be received while the insured person is alive and continues to be totally disabled.
Proof of total disability must be provided within 3 months of the notice being given.
Premiums falling due more than 12 months before notice of total disability will not be waived.
We may request proof of continued total disability at any time.
If proof of continued total disability is not provided, the insured person will be deemed no longer totally disabled as of the day before the request was made.
Monthly premiums will resume after the insured person is deemed no longer totally disabled.

TD 20-Year Term Life Insurance is a type of life insurance policy.
The policy provides coverage for a 20-year period.
Term life insurance offers a death benefit if the insured passes away during the term.
The policy does not accumulate cash value.
Premiums are typically fixed for the duration of the 20-year term.
It is designed to provide financial protection for a specific period.
The death benefit is paid to the designated beneficiary upon the insured's death.
The policy expires at the end of the 20-year term if the insured is still alive.
You may convert the insurance in effect under this policy at any time up to and including the policy anniversary nearest to the insured person's 69th birthday.
The conversion option is available regardless of the health of the insured person.
The converted insurance will be for an amount not to exceed the Sum Insured provided on the Policy Schedule.
The converted insurance will be on any permanent plan that the insurer makes available for such conversion.
To exercise the conversion option, you must complete an application form provided by the insurer.
To exercise the conversion option, you must pay the first premium required for the converted insurance.
The amount of the first premium depends on the age of the insured person at the time of conversion.
The amount of the first premium depends on the risk classification used for this policy.
The amount of the first premium depends on the plan of the converted insurance.
The converted policy will not include any waiver of premium benefits that were present on the original policy.

TD 20-Year Term Life insurance provides life insurance protection for a period of 20 years.
The policy offers a fixed premium rate for the entire 20-year term.
Death benefits are paid to the designated beneficiaries if the insured passes away during the 20-year term.
Term life insurance does not accumulate cash value over time.
The coverage amount remains constant throughout the 20-year term.
The policy expires at the end of the 20-year term if the insured is still alive.
TD offers this insurance product as part of its life insurance offerings.
The policy is designed to provide financial protection for dependents during a specific period.

Beneficiary means the person or persons named in writing to receive all benefits under this policy.
Effective Date means the date(s) the policy goes into effect, as shown in the Policy Schedule.

If the full premium is paid during the grace period, the premium is considered to have been paid on time.
Lapse refers to the termination of this policy because the premium was not paid within the grace period.
Lapse Date refers to the date the policy lapses.
Insured person is the person whose life is insured under this policy.
Policy means this document which provides evidence of your insurance.
Policy Owner is the person or party who owns this individual insurance policy, and is not necessarily the person whose life is insured.
Reinstatement is the restoration of a lapsed policy as described in the section 'Putting your policy back into effect'.
Reinstatement Effective Date is the date this policy is reinstated as described in the section 'Putting your policy back into effect'.

The policy includes a conversion option.
The conversion option can be exercised while the insured person is totally disabled.
If the conversion option is exercised during total disability, premiums will not be waived.
The converted insurance policy does not receive premium waivers under this condition.
The policy allows an advance payment of up to 50% of the Death Benefit if the insured has a terminal illness.
The maximum advance payment for the Living Benefit is $100,000.
The insured must have a terminal illness with a life expectancy of 12 months or less to qualify for the Living Benefit.
The policy must be in force and paid current to be eligible for the Living Benefit.
The Living Benefit is paid directly to the insured person.
An irrevocable beneficiary or policy owner must consent to the payment of the Living Benefit.
A special collateral assignment arrangement is used to assign the policy to the insurer when the benefit is approved and paid.
The Living Benefit is administered as a loan, which makes it non-taxable in most instances.
Once the Living Benefit is claimed, the policy owner may not cancel the policy.
Unpaid premiums will increase the amount of the Living Benefit if not paid.
Both the Living Benefit amount and any unpaid premiums accumulate interest until the death of the insured.
Interest on the accumulated amounts is set at the current prime rate and may be adjusted if the prime rate changes by 1% or more.
The owner of the insurance coverage is the only person entitled to change the beneficiary.
The ability to change the beneficiary applies whether the coverage is for the individual or for the entire family.
Consent from any previously designated irrevocable beneficiaries must be obtained when changing the beneficiary.
Irrevocable beneficiaries have a protected status that requires their consent for any change.
Under current tax law, benefits paid on a life insurance policy are tax-free to the designated beneficiary.
If you are the policy owner, you may add a Waiver of Premium Benefit to your TD 20-Year Term Life insurance policy for an additional cost.
The Waiver of Premium Benefit must be added at the time of purchasing the TD 20-Year Term Life insurance policy.
The Waiver of Premium Benefit ensures that your coverage remains in place if you become disabled and cannot return to work.
The Waiver of Premium Benefit may help if you have difficulty paying for the policy due to disability.
Life insurance can help provide financial security for your family in the event of your death.
More Canadians are surviving critical illnesses such as cancer, heart attack, or stroke due to medical breakthroughs.
Financial help may be needed to protect your lifestyle during recovery from a critical illness or accident.
TD Insurance offers the Critical Illness Recovery Plan and Critical Accident Recovery Plan to support individuals after critical events.

The form must be signed and dated by the owner.

If the policy owner is a corporation, a corporate seal is required with the signing officer's signature.
If a corporate seal is not available, the signatures of two signing officers with their titles are acceptable.
The form must be witnessed by someone who is not related to the signer and not named as a beneficiary.
The 'Request for Change of Beneficiary' form must be mailed to TD Life using the enclosed self-addressed return envelope.
TD Life will validate and record the change of beneficiary and return a confirmation letter.
TD Life cannot guarantee the legal effectiveness of any change of beneficiary.
Beneficiaries can be designated as either revocable or irrevocable.
An irrevocable beneficiary must consent to any future changes to the policy, including changing or surrendering the policy.
In Quebec, a spouse is automatically considered an irrevocable beneficiary unless explicitly designated as revocable.
To change an irrevocable minor beneficiary designation, the minor must reach the age of majority (18 or older, depending on jurisdiction).
Until reaching the age of majority, neither the legal guardian nor the minor can sign a release of the irrevocable designation.
A trustee cannot sign a release of an irrevocable minor beneficiary designation because they do not have entitlement prior to the death of the insured.
Revoking a minor child's irrevocable beneficiary designation requires a court order.
Proceeds payable to a named beneficiary (other than 'estate') are paid directly to the beneficiary and do not go through the estate.
Proceeds paid directly to a beneficiary are not subject to probate or executor fees.
Estate creditors cannot make claims against proceeds paid directly to a named beneficiary.
Beneficiaries receive proceeds as soon as the claim is approved, without waiting for estate settlement.
Estate settlement can take a few months to several years, depending on circumstances.
The policyholder decides who receives the insurance proceeds.
If proceeds are payable to 'Estate', they are distributed according to provincial intestacy laws if there is no will.
A contingent beneficiary is a secondary beneficiary who receives proceeds if no primary beneficiaries are alive at the time of death.
In Quebec, a contingent beneficiary is referred to as a 'subrogated' beneficiary.
An irrevocable beneficiary designation means the policyholder cannot change the beneficiary without the irrevocable beneficiary's consent.
An irrevocable beneficiary must consent to any changes to the policy, including surrendering it.
A minor is defined as a person who has not reached the age of majority and thus has limited contractual capacity.
A primary beneficiary is the individual or individuals who receive the insurance proceeds upon the policyholder's death.
The claim for insurance benefits must be made within 365 days of the date of death of the insured person.
If the claim is not made within 365 days of the date of death, no insurance benefit will be paid.
If the insured person dies due to suicide within 2 years of the effective date of coverage, no insurance benefit will be paid.
If the insured person dies due to suicide within 2 years of the most recent reinstatement effective date, no insurance benefit will be paid.
Suicide includes cases where the insured person is aware or unaware of the result of their actions.
The state of mind of the insured person at the time of suicide is irrelevant for determining benefit payment.
If no insurance benefit is payable due to suicide, the policy owner will receive 100% of premiums paid since the effective date of coverage.
If the policy has been reinstated, the policy owner will receive 100% of premiums paid since the most recent reinstatement effective date.

All coverage under this policy will end on the earliest of the date when the insured person dies, a request to cancel coverage is received, the grace period ends due to unpaid premiums, a fraudulent claim is made, or the insured person reaches their 80th birthday.
The policy will be renewed for successive 20-year periods after the initial 20-year period.
The renewal period nearest to the insured person's 80th birthday will be shorter and will end on the insured person's 80th birthday.
The final renewal period will end exactly on the insured person's 80th birthday, with no further renewals possible.
If a benefit is payable, it will be paid to the designated beneficiary(ies).
If there is no beneficiary at the time of the insured person's death, the benefit will be paid to the policyholder or their estate.
The right to name or change the beneficiary is reserved solely to the policyholder.
The policyholder does not need the consent of any beneficiary to change the beneficiary designation, unless required by law.
Beneficiary designations are revocable unless otherwise stated or unless contrary law applies.
To name or change a beneficiary, a written request must be submitted to the insurer.
A beneficiary change request is not binding until it is received at the insurer's Head Office.
Once received, the insurer will act as if the change was effective from the date the request was signed.
The insurer is not responsible for benefits paid to the previous beneficiary before the request was received.

The policy premium payment frequency will automatically change to monthly if it is not already monthly.
The change to monthly payment frequency begins on the date premiums are agreed to be waived.
After the end of total disability, the insured person can request to pay premiums in a frequency other than monthly, but must notify the insurer.
Monthly premiums will be waived from the date the insured person becomes totally disabled.
Premium waivers continue for as long as the total disability lasts, up to the insured person's 80th birthday.
Total disability means the insured person is wholly prevented from performing the substantial duties of their own occupation for the first two years after injury or disease onset.

After the first two years, total disability means the insured person is prevented from engaging in any gainful occupation for which they are or may reasonably become qualified by training, education, or experience.
Coverage is available for your spouse and other family members.
The death of a spouse or partner could cause financial hardship.
Financial hardship may include childcare expenses.
Financial hardship may include medical bills.
Financial hardship may include funeral expenses.
Spouses, partners, or other family members may consider purchasing TD 20-year Term Life insurance.
TD 20-year Term Life insurance could help ensure money is available to maintain your lifestyle.
TD 20-year Term Life insurance could help ensure money is available to raise your family.
If a policy lapsed, it may be reinstated if the insured person is alive.
Reinstatement is the process of putting a lapsed policy back into effect.
An application for reinstatement must be submitted within 2 years of the lapse date.
To reinstate a policy, the insured person must be alive.
All unpaid premiums from the lapse date up to the reinstatement effective date must be paid.
A completed Reinstatement Application must be provided.
New evidence of insurability for the insured person must be provided and considered satisfactory by the insurer.

The proof of death must be satisfactory to the insurer.

The Primary Beneficiary section requires the First, Middle, and Last Name, Date of Birth or Age, Percent of Benefit to be paid in the event of death, and whether the proceeds will be Revocable or Irrevocable.
If a secondary Contingent Beneficiary is designated, the form requires the same information as the Primary Beneficiary after the phrase 'if living, otherwise to'.
It is recommended to name a Trustee when designating a child or children as a Primary or Contingent Beneficiary.

If a trustee is designated for minor children, the form requires the First, Middle, and Last Name, Date of Birth or Age, Percent of Benefit to be paid in the event of passing, and whether the proceeds will be Revocable or Irrevocable.
The information for each beneficiary must be provided on page 2 of the form if a trustee is being named for minor children.
The form specifies that the proceeds for each beneficiary can be designated as either Revocable or Irrevocable.
The Percent of Benefit to be paid in the event of death must be specified for each named beneficiary.
The form includes definitions for Primary Beneficiary, Contingent Beneficiary, and Trustee that are referenced in the instructions.
TD 20-Year Term Life insurance provides a tax-free, lump sum benefit if the insured dies.
The insurance coverage can help cover tuition costs for children's education.
The benefit can help pay down a mortgage to allow the family to remain in their home.
The insurance can help cover funeral expenses, reducing financial burden on the family.
The policy can provide funds for day-to-day living expenses after the insured's death.
The policy can provide extra money to pay medical bills or outstanding debts.
Premiums for TD 20-Year Term Life insurance are fixed for the 20-year term and guaranteed not to increase during that period.
Premiums increase at each renewal of the 20-year term.
Coverage renews automatically every 20 years until the insured turns 80, provided premiums are paid.
There is no medical examination required for automatic renewals.
You can convert to permanent life insurance at any time up to and including the policy anniversary nearest the insured's 69th birthday.
Conversion to permanent life insurance does not require a medical examination.
Premiums on the converted permanent policy are based on the insured's age at the time of conversion.
The terms 'TD Life' and 'reinsurers' include those acting on their behalf.
A reproduction of this authorization is as valid as the original.
This authorization remains valid as long as the individual remains a client of TD Life.
By applying for TD 20-Year Term Life insurance, the applicant agrees that insurers and reinsurers may collect, use, and disclose their information as described in the Privacy Agreement.
Insurers and reinsurers may use personal information to identify the individual, provide ongoing service, and meet legal requirements.

Any licensed physician, medical practitioner, hospital, clinic, medical facility, insurance company, MIB, Inc., or other organization with knowledge of the individual’s health may release information to TD Life or its reinsurers.
TD Life, its authorized administrators, or reinsurers may make a brief report of personal health information to the MIB, a non-profit membership organization of life insurance companies.
The MIB operates an information exchange on behalf of its member life insurance companies.
In the event of a claim, heirs and the executor or administrator of the estate are authorized to provide TD Life with all necessary information and authorizations for claims purposes.
TD Life may share non-health personal information with its affiliates to offer products and services via telephone, internet, mail, or other methods.
Individuals may choose not to be contacted for direct marketing offers by informing TD Life.
The applicant confirms they understand and agree to the terms and conditions regarding the use of information.
